title: Rat faeces virus leaves three dead on cruise ship in the Atlantic
text:
Three people have died and another is in intensive care in South Africa after a suspected outbreak of the rare but deadly hantavirus on a cruise ship sailing in the Atlantic Ocean.

The World Health Organisation (WHO) said that of the six people on the ship exhibiting symptoms, only one of the cases had been confirmed on the vessel, a Dutch cruise liner called the MV Hondius.

Oceanwide Expeditions, which operates the ship, confirmed it was currently off the coast of Cape Verde in West Africa , and said two crew members on board required "urgent attention."

The cruise ship MV Hondius stationary off the port of Praia, the capital of Cape Verde, on May 3, 2025. An outbreak of "severe acute respiratory illness" on board a cruise ship in the Atlantic has left three people dead, a third in intensive care in Johannesburg, South Africa, and two crew members in need of urgent medical attention. The outbreak occurred on the MV Hondius, travelling from Ushuaia in Argentina to Cape Verde. (AFP)

﻿It said Cape Verdean health authorities had boarded the ship to assess the people requiring medical attention, but that they were not allowed to leave the ship.

"No authorisation from Cape Verdean authorities has been given to disembark guests requiring medical care or to support with medical screening," Oceanwide Expeditions said in a statement released this morning.

"Local health authorities have visited the vessel to assess the condition of the two symptomatic individuals."

The company also said it was working with Dutch authorities to repatriate two symptomatic people on the ship back to the Netherlands, along with the body of one of the ﻿people who died on board.

The company, in a post on social media, said they were facing "complex challenges" to help expedite the treatment of its sick crew members.﻿

The ship was sailing from Ushuaia at the bottom of Argentina to Cape Verde, and is estimated to have 150 passengers on board, according to South Africa's health department.

Nine.com.au has reached out to the Department of Foreign Affairs and Trade ﻿to see if any Australians are on board.

Several online tour operators said the Hondius, which is described as a specialist polar cruise ship, usually travels with around 70 crew members.

The first victim was a 70-year-old man who died on the ship and whose body was removed in the British territory of Saint Helena in the South Atlantic, South Africa's Department of Health said in a statement.

The man's wife collapsed at an airport in South Africa trying to take a flight to her home country of the Netherlands, the department said. She died at a nearby hospital.

The department identified the patient in intensive care in a hospital in Johannesburg as a British national.

It said that the person fell ill near Ascension Island, another remote island in the Atlantic, after the ship left Saint Helena and was transferred from there to South Africa.

The MV Hondius. (AFP/Getty)

South Africa's National Institute for Communicable Diseases, meanwhile, was conducting contact tracing in the Johannesburg region to identify if other people were exposed to the infected passengers in South Africa.

People can become infected with Hantavirus through exposure to rodents' urine or faeces, and the disease can also spread from person to person, though this is rare.

Initial symptoms include fatigue, fever and muscle aches, but it can also lead to severe respiratory illnesses and death.

"Detailed investigations are ongoing, including further laboratory testing, and epidemiological investigations. Medical care and support are being provided to passengers and crew," WHO said in a statement published on X.

What is hantavirus?

﻿Hantavirus are a group of viruses that can impact people, though there have been no human cases recorded in Australia.

The virus is found all over the world, including in Asia, Europe and the USA.

It gained attention last year ﻿ after the late actor Gene Hackman's wife, Betsy Arakawa, died from hantavirus infection.

Actor Gene Hackman and his Betsy Arakawa in 2003. The pair died last year, with Arakawa sufffering from hantavirus, it was later rveealed. (AP)

A photo taken by investigators during the search of the home of Gene Hackman and his wife Betsy Arakawa. (Santa Fe County Sheriff)

Hackman died around a week later at their home from heart disease.

The virus can cause a severe and sometimes deadly lung infection called hantavirus pulmonary syndrome, according to the US Centres for Disease Control and Prevention.

There is no specific treatment or cure for hantavirus infections, but early medical attention can increase the chance of survival.

Reported with Associated Press.

title: Millions in path of wintry blast this week
text:
A﻿ spell of warm and sunny weather across south-eastern Australia is forecast to come to a shuddering halt this week.

Tasmania, Victoria and southern NSW will feel much chillier by Thursday when a blast of cold winds from the deep Southern Ocean rolls in, reports weather data firm Weatherzone .

The weather system will begin its march on Wednesday, and over the following 24 hours, snowfalls can be expected in parts of the NSW Snowy Mountains, the Victorian Alps and the Tasmanian Highlands.

Gusty winds over south-eastern states on Thursday, May 7, will accompany the plunging temperatures. (Windy.com) (Supplied)

The latter two could record falls as low as 800 metres, forecasters say.

A second cold front is due to arrive early Friday, extending the plummeting temperatures and gusty winds through the day, while snowfalls will be limited to higher ground.

But skiers and snowboarders shouldn't expect an early start to the winter sports season.

"Snow accumulations across the two days should amount to approximately 10-20cm across the Victorian and New South Wales major resorts and about 3-8cm across the Tasmanian Central Plateau and the higher peaks in south-west Victoria," according to Weatherzone.

"This should be ample snow cover for snow play, snowball fights and maybe a slide on a toboggan.﻿"

Alpine regions in Tasmania, Victoria and NSW can expect snowfalls later this week. (Thredbo Resort)

Residents in south-eastern capital cities should be reaching for the winter woollies by Thursday when the mercury drops markedly.

Canberra is heading for a chilly maximum temperature of 8 degrees, Hobart 11 degrees, Melbourne 12 degrees, while Sydney can expect a far more pleasant 19 degrees.

Anyone heading outdoors should prepare to add an extra layer of clothing, with cold, gusty winds accentuating the chill factor.

The wintry blast is forecast to be brief, with seasonal average temperatures returning ﻿by the late weekend.

title: 'Motivation to swim faster': Crocodile bites four people before swimming race
text:
A man in his 60s is in a stable condition in a Perth hospital after being bitten by a suspected freshwater crocodile in Western Australia's north on Friday evening.

The man, who was on a houseboat on Lake Argyle with a group, was bitten while swimming at the Hicks Passage area of the lake, 3,000 kilometres north of Perth.

Three other people were also bitten by the same crocodile while trying to help the man.

The man in his 60s was flown to Royal Perth Hospital with non-life-threatening injuries.

More than 400 swimmers took part in the Lake Argyle Swim competition on Saturday. (Supplied: Timbee Photography)

Lake Argyle Swim goes ahead

The incident did not deter more than 400 swimmers who plunged into the lake on Saturday as part of the sold-out Lake Argyle Swim competition.

Event manager Glenn Taylor said while the route was changed for safety reasons, no swimmers pulled out of the competition.

"We sprung into action straight away because the safety of our swimmers is paramount," he said.

"We informed all the teams they'd be going a different 20km course, not going near that area.

" We didn't get one swimmer who expressed any concerns and certainly no-one that did not take part in the swim because of the news. "

'Motivation to swim faster'

Lake Argyle is home to about 35,000 freshwater crocodiles, which are less aggressive than their saltwater counterparts.

But the species can still attack when provoked, with a family dog narrowly escaping an attack near the lake last month.

A swimmer sustained serious leg injuries in 2022.

Ultra swimmer Andy Donaldson, who last week broke a world record by swimming 55km in less than 12 hours, said he felt in good hands on Saturday.

"It's always there in some corner of your mind, " he said.

" We had the team out there, so many boats, a lot of water safety so I felt like I was in very safe hands. "

Local swimmer Ben "Broady" Broadwith, who has been training in Lake Argyle since 2011, said freshwater crocodiles were not something that concerned him.

"I grew up in Lake Argyle … we don't have a pool at the moment so we train in Lake Argyle," he said.

"It's where I'm most comfortable. It's a very safe body of water to swim in."

A freshwater crocodile bite did not deter a record number of people competing in the Lake Argyle Swim on Saturday. (Supplied: Timbee Photography)

Darwin Swimming Club secretary Valerie Smith, whose team broke a record for the 20km quad competition on Saturday, said the organisers responded appropriately to the attack on Friday by redirecting swimmers.

"We're from the top end of Australia and used to crocs. We were aware it was a freshie," she said.

"They actually saw freshies last year when they were swimming at the event.

"It maybe gave them a bit of motivation to swim faster."

The Department of Biodiversity, Conservation and Attractions said it was continuing to monitor the situation and would be conducting patrols in the area this week.

title: Socceroos sweat on mainstay's injury blow ahead of World Cup
text:
The Socceroos will nervously await news on Aiden O'Neill after the industrious midfield regular suffered an ankle injury less than six weeks out from the World Cup.

After being stepped on, O'Neill went down twice, then came off the field just 36 minutes into New York City FC's 2-0 loss to DC United on Sunday, local time.

The news will send waves through Socceroos camp, given O'Neill, looking to play in his first World Cup finals, has been coach Tony Popovic's first-choice defensive midfielder along with Jackson Irvine.

Toure scores again for Norwich Mohamed Toure scores his 10th goal in just 12 matches for Norwich to keep him in red hot form ahead of the World Cup.

More recently, the likes of Paul Okon Jnr and Max Balard have also paired up with O'Neill, 27, who has started Australia's past seven games and has 29 caps.

The Socceroos will hope O'Neill's injury is on the less serious end of the scale, given the short lead-in to the World Cup, with Australia's first game on Sunday, June 14.

O'Neill's NYCFC coach confirmed, much like Irvine, he had already been playing with an ankle problem.

Aiden O'Neill has played every game this season in the MLS for New York City FC. (AP: Nell Redmond) "Yeah, he has an ankle issue," Pascal Jensen said.

"But he plays with some issues in his ankle all the time. He doesn't know any better, in fact. So that is something that we're aware of.

"But he had a moment in the game, I think it was five or six minutes before we took him off, he had a moment where he tried to turn and Aiden is a player that plays on the half turn always, either left or right, doesn't matter.

"But his ankle just locked and had too much pain to continue. He tried for a couple minutes, and I know for a fact, that Aido is not a guy that just steps out unnecessarily."

ABC Sport Daily podcast ABC Sport Daily is your daily sports conversation. We dive into the biggest story of the day and get you up to speed with everything else that's making headlines.

O'Neill's teammate Kai Trewin played a full game, as did fellow Aussie defender Kye Rowles for DC United.

Popovic is due to speak to media on Monday afternoon in Sydney.

The Socceroos have a training camp in Sarasota, then play a friendly against Mexico in Los Angeles on May 31 — the last game before Popovic selects his squad on June 1 — before relocating to their Bay Area base.

Australia then play Switzerland on June 7 in San Diego, their final warm-up game.

The Socceroos face Turkey in Vancouver on June 14, the United States in Seattle six days later, then Paraguay in Santa Clara on June 26 (all dates AEST).

AAP

title: China accused of targeting Port Pirie and Hobart metal smelters
text:
South Australia's treasurer and energy minister says China is undermining Australia's smelting industry and that any shutdown would leave national security "very exposed".

Tom Koutsantonis made the claims on 891 ABC Adelaide when asked about the future of Nyrstar's troubled lead smelter in Port Pirie.

"I think you should be concerned anytime another government to our north, like China, is doing everything it can to use its taxpayers' money to bankrupt this smelter," he said.

"Make no mistake about it, this smelter is under attack. This smelter is having every aspect of its commerciality undermined by a foreign government."

Tom Koutsantonis says the profitability of Port Pirie's smelter is "under attack". (ABC News: Justin Hewitson)

Asked how China was "undermining" the smelter, Mr Koutsantonis said: "They're buying raw materials at an elevated price, they are discounting the charges for making the materials, and then they subsidise the manufacture of it."

"At every stage of the operations of antimony and other products that go towards making antimony, which are lead and zinc and everything else we use at the smelter, the Chinese government are doing everything they can to build their state sovereignty around it," he said.

"That's fine for them, but for us, in an emergency, if we needed to be able to defend ourselves and use these critical metals to harden our bullets, harden our bombs, harden our armour in our military applications and we have to buy all of it entirely from one country … you would leave Australia very, very exposed."

Industry viability threatened

Tasmanian Treasurer Eric Abetz said China was making it difficult for smelters in the Western world to be able to compete.

He said some of Nyrstar's difficulties stemmed from China purchasing ore at spot prices.

"They are then providing the finished product onto world markets with subsidised prices as a result, making it very difficult for smelters in the Western world who operate on a private enterprise system to be able to effectively compete," Mr Abetz said.

Peter Malinauskas was in Port Pirie last year discussing the future of Nyrstar's lead smelter. (ABC News: Trent Murphy)

SA Premier Peter Malinauskas said China was making it "very hard" for Nyrstar to remain viable.

"Smelting globally in Western countries with Western country cost bases that aren't being subsidised by governments are uneconomic, that's it," he said.

"That's the play. You can read about this in a whole range of published journals.

"The Chinese government is very deliberately subsidising their smelting capabilities with the view of reducing treatment charges globally which are the source of revenue for our smelters, to the extent that our smelters become uncommercial and close."

Mr Malinauskas said in other parts of the world, including Europe and North America, smelters had closed because they were "uneconomic as a result of Chinese subsidies to Chinese smelters".

"We can't let that undo our smelting capability as a country," he said.

The Chinese government has been approached for comment.

Smelter's struggling finances

Nyrstar Australia has facilities in Hobart and Port Pirie.

The South Australian, Tasmanian and federal governments had to step in last August with a $135 million bailout for the struggling smelters after Nyrstar revealed its operations were losing tens of millions of dollars a month.

In March 2025 Nyrstar's parent company, Trafigura, told an international business summit its Australian smelters were "uncompetitive assets" that "shouldn't be in fully private hands".

About 1,400 jobs will be on the line if the Port Pirie and Nyrstar smelters close down.

On Sunday a Nyrstar spokesperson confirmed it had received a new support offer from the state and federal governments, but declined to provide a timeline on when the company would respond.

Nyrstar says its zinc smelter in Hobart is one of the largest in the world. (ABC News: Maren Preuss)

Tasmanian Business, Industry and Resources Minister Felix Ellis said the state government's contribution would be $7.5 million.

Mr Ellis told 936 ABC Hobart the "lion's share" of financial assistance would come from SA and the federal government.

He did not say over what period the money would be delivered because negotiations were continuing.

"Really it's about making sure that we can get these operations from where they are, which is a challenged financial position like we're seeing with smelters across the country and many parts of the Western world, through to the other side and viability," Mr Ellis said.

"There's a lot of good faith between the three governments and the company to work towards a sustainable future here."

Mr Malinauskas said negotiations between Trafigura, the federal government and the two state governments "continue in earnest".

He said smelting capacity was critical to Australia's sovereignty and its ability to participate in the critical mineral and metal supply chain.

"The federal government needs to be all in on this," he said.

"Let's not make the same mistake with smelting that the country made with fuel refining 15-20 years ago.

"If we lose it, it's gone, and it's very hard to bring it back."

Detail on deal lacking

Australian Workers' Union (AWU) assistant secretary Robert Flanagan told 936 ABC Hobart he had little detail around what the offer contained.

He said the AWU had had discussions with the smelter locally.

"They're not able to go into any of the details, other than to say there was a revised offer that the company had received on Friday afternoon and that the company would consider their position," Mr Flanagan said.

He said there was still some anxiety among the workforce, but that "like the union, the workers are hoping this will be resolved sooner rather than later".

title: Landlords to lose ability to evict without reason under WA reforms
text:
No-grounds evictions will be banned in Western Australia in what the state government is describing as a "commonsense approach" to addressing a tight housing market.

Under the proposed laws, landlords will no longer be allowed to terminate a tenant's lease without giving a reason.

WA and the Northern Territory are the only jurisdictions in Australia that still allow no-grounds evictions.

Commerce Minister and Attorney-General Tony Buti said it was important to have a "fair balance" between landlords and tenants.

"The market is a very tight market. People are feeling pressure. Tenants need security," he told 102.5 ABC Perth.

Tony Buti says the WA housing market is "very tight". (ABC News: Keane Bourke)

Dr Buti described the experience of a single mother who said she was struggling with the risk of being evicted without grounds.

"This was a single mother who had a severely disabled child, she was living in rental accommodation, [with] sub-par standards," he said.

"It was so bad that the NDIS providers, to assist her child, would not come into the house.

" But she was too afraid to address the standard, the living conditions of her rental property because she thought that the landlord would bring her lease to an end. "

The WA government is also extending the state's one-off rent relief payments for tenants at risk of eviction.

Policy shift

The banning of no-grounds evictions is a marked shift in policy for the state government.

'We're not ready to leave': Inside the messy dynamic of a no-grounds eviction Evicting a tenant without cause offers landlords a simple way out, but the issue can be much more complex than it appears. One such example of that complexity is Eleanor Kahlo's case of her no-grounds eviction.

Former premier Mark McGowan and commerce minister Sue Ellery previously argued that outlawing the practice could drive investment away from the state and compound housing supply issues.

"We're just ensuring that we have a commonsense approach," Dr Buti said.

"Ensuring there's a balance between the rights and obligations of landlords and tenants."

Former renter Le, who did not want her last name revealed for privacy reasons, was given a no-grounds lease termination notice last year, after her landlord raised the rent by $50 a week.

Le questioned a clause in her new contract that would prevent her from being home during inspections.

Le was evicted from her home on no grounds last year. (ABC News: Cason Ho)

"Five days later, I got the termination notice, and it gave me 30 days to get out," she said.

"Many people have it worse than I have, but it's stressful for all of us. Every single person who's renting really just has no stability.

Le feels the announcement will have a big impact on renters. (ABC News: Cason Ho)

"I think there's more changes that need to happen to allow people to rent and be comfortable, but I think it's a really good step in the right way."

In Margaret River, where the median rent has reached $900 per week, housing support worker Shona Hunter was jubilant when she heard the news.

She said no-grounds evictions, coupled with the tourist region's critical shortage of rental properties and an abundance of short-stay accommodation, had left tenants significantly disadvantaged.

Just Home coordinator Shona Hunter was relieved when she heard the news. (ABC South West WA: Georgia Loney)

"It takes a lot of bravery to speak out and ask for something like mould to be fixed, or asbestos to be removed," Ms Hunter said.

"People are scared to speak up because there are so few rentals out there."

Landlords will still be allowed to evict tenants for specific reasons, for example, for damaging a property, or for consistently failing to pay rent.

Industry concerns

Real Estate Institute of WA (REIWA) president Suzanne Brown said she was concerned about the announcement's impact on investments in the private housing market.

"Our main concern is that we feel it will further impact supply," she told 102.5 ABC Perth.

" We've already seen a mass exodus of investors post-COVID, and we're still operating below the supply peak that we hit in 2021, so we're dealing with five years of really low supply. "

But Anglicare WA chief executive Mark Glasson said there was little evidence nationally that outlawing no-grounds evictions impacted private investments.

Anglicare CEO Mark Glasson. (ABC News: Lauren Smith)

"You've got plenty of grounds still available to evict people who are problematic tenants," he said.

"We know that the use of no-grounds evictions has been weaponised by some landlords ... and it's also prevented people from asking for reasonable improvements to their houses."

Speaking at a press conference later on Monday, Dr Buti said there had been enough time to monitor how other state and territory markets reacted to their bans.

"We've closely examined what's happened in other states when they've removed no-grounds termination, and it appears the market hasn't been affected."

WA Greens housing spokesperson Tim Clifford said the reform was a "huge step forward in improving rights for renters", but argued there were still gaps which needed to be addressed.

"For so many WA families, paying their rent is the single biggest expense in their weekly budget and it is still going up … they can still be evicted via an unaffordable rent increase," he said.

"It is incredibly disappointing that the Cook Labor government still won't commit to capping rent increases, which would give even more security to renters."

title: Victorian teachers not to strike, council workers proceed with 'historic' action
text:
Rolling teacher strikes that were due to start this week have been called off, with the union saying there have been positive developments with its negotiations for a new pay deal.

Tens of thousands of teachers walked off the job for 24-hours in March in the first strike of its kind in 13 years, as they pursued a 35 per cent pay rise over four years.

About 35-000 Victorian teachers, principals and education support staff took part in the March strike. (Supplied: Emily Pinney Brown)

Union members were due to begin half-day strikes region-by-region this week to coincide with the Victorian government releasing the 2026/27 state budget.

But the Australian Education Union (AEU) says negotiations have progressed and, in response, it has suspended the strikes for two weeks.

"For the AEU, this means closing the percentage gap between Victorian wages and those of public education workers in NSW in 2026, with further strong increases in subsequent years," Victorian branch president Justin Mullaly said in a statement.

The union has not revealed what the state government offered to trigger the suspension, but Mr Mullaly said calling off the strikes would allow intensive negotiations to continue and provide the best chance to reach an agreement.

However, other industrial bans will continue, including refusing to answer Department of Education emails, not providing written comments in student reports and banning state Labor MPs from visiting schools.

Last week, Education Minister Ben Carroll denied reports that the teachers had been offered a 28 per cent pay rise.

The union also said that figure had not been put forward in negotiations.

Teachers are calling for a pay rise in line with their counterparts in other states. (ABC News: Danielle Bonica)

Premier Jacinta Allan welcomed the suspension of the strikes.

"Families would be relieved to see that and it's a demonstration that the negotiations have been going strongly," she said.

"The government's negotiating position has always been that teachers deserve a pay rise, and a pay rise in line with nationally-consistent settings."

A spokesperson said an eight-hour bargaining meeting was continuing today.

'Historic' council strike to affect Melbourne suburbs

Meanwhile, a 24-hour strike by workers at eight Melbourne councils will go ahead tomorrow on budget day.

More than a thousand Australian Services Union (ASU) members are expected to walk off the job as part of their push for a pay rise and improved work conditions.

The councils affected are Darebin, Greater Dandenong, Hobsons Bay, Hume, Maribyrnong, Melbourne, Merri-bek and Yarra.

"ASU members at eight metropolitan councils will escalate their fight for fair pay on Tuesday, with an historic 24-hour strike and rally at Parliament House," the union said.

Workers last month refused to empty wheelie bins or issue parking fines as the pay dispute intensified.

The union accused the state government of underfunding councils and maintaining a cap on rates, which it said had suppressed workers' wages and reduced the ability to deliver essential services to ratepayers and residents.

In a separate dispute with the state government, some members of the Health Services union have escalated industrial action.

Medical scientists, psychologists, hospital pharmacists and dietitians have since last week refused to perform unpaid overtime or extra duties not explicitly required of their role.

The work-to-rule action follows more than a year of bargaining with the government over pay and conditions.

title: Worker shortage sees people struggle in 'most extreme form of homelessness'
text:
Victoria needs almost five times as many outreach field workers to help people who are sleeping rough, according to a report from the Council to Homeless Persons (CHP).

The report, titled Mapping Assertive Outreach, found there were 10,626 rough sleepers who attempted to access support services last year, a 25 per cent increase in the past five years.

CHP estimated Victoria needs at least 177 full-time assertive outreach workers, who locate and assist rough sleepers, to meet demand.

Currently there are 37 full-time assertive outreach workers in Victoria, 26 of which are are government funded.

CHP chief executive officer Deborah Di Natale said about 55 local government areas had no assertive outreach, including Shepparton, Latrobe, Mitchell, Whittlesea and Wodonga shires.

Deborah Di Natale is calling on the Victorian government to fix the homelessness outreach supply problem. (ABC News: Terry McDonald)

She said one outreach worker in the Wimmera was expected to travel 34,000 square kilometres across five local government areas.

"What that tells us is we've got one worker who is not going to be able to get to everybody that he needs to get to," Ms Di Natale said.

"In Gippsland, we don't have a single assertive outreach worker.

" People are doing it tough. "

The report stated there were no dedicated assertive outreach programs anywhere in Victoria for young people sleeping rough alone.

'It can be heartbreaking'

Glenn Kimberley is the sole outreach field worker in the Wimmera that Ms Di Natale mentioned.

He works for Uniting Wimmera in a philanthropically funded position.

Glenn Kimberley is the Wimmera's only outreach field worker. (Supplied: Glenn Kimberley)

Mr Kimberley said his work put him in touch with people enduring "the most extreme form of homelessness".

"People living in cars, derelict buildings, tents, swags, disabled toilets, parks and shop fronts … my role is to go on outreach and find these people," he said.

"These people don't come looking for help, whether that's shame, pride or just being let down by the system."

He said there were often places he could not reach, but where help was needed.

"It can be heartbreaking," he said.

"I know there's people out there who need assistance and they're not getting it."

'I never thought I'd become homeless'

One of Mr Kimberley's clients is Sarah (named changed for privacy reasons), a woman aged in her 50s.

"Honestly, I never though I'd become homeless and I did," she said.

After enduring family tragedy, Sarah struggled to keep up with rent.

She was eventually evicted and left with nowhere else to go.

Sarah says the outreach program changed her life. (ABC News: Sharon Gordon)

"Rent is too high for a single person by themselves," Sarah said.

She was residing in a shed with no bathroom when she met Mr Kimberley.

"Glenn [Mr Kimberley] did everything within his power to find somewhere I could safely be," Sarah said.

" I don't know where I would have been without that. "

Mr Kimberley secured Sarah two stints of emergency accommodation within weeks of their first meeting before finding her ongoing commission housing.

'Not a luxury'

Ms Di Natale said assertive outreach was "not a luxury" but an essential part of the state government's homeless response.

"Every single person with lived experience who's been sleeping rough that I've met has said to me the single biggest intervention between sleeping rough and ending up in a home has been the impact of having an assertive outreach worker come and develop trust, and get them back into the system," she said.

The CHP report called on the Victorian government to expand existing state-funded programs and establish new ones in areas that were lacking adequate support.

Deborah Di Natale wants better outcomes for rough sleepers. (ABC News: Danielle Bonica)

It also recommended long-term funding to keep experienced staff in their current roles, give outreach teams priority access to social housing, build more social housing with dedicated spots for rough sleepers and pilot an assertive outreach program for young people.

The Victorian government has been contacted for comment.

title: Qld ministers clarify personal relationship timeline amid integrity questions
text:
Two Queensland ministers have been forced to clarify the nature and timeline of their personal relationship amid questions over their appointments to cabinet.

The state opposition claimed on Saturday that the government had an "integrity crisis" stemming from the relationship between Sport and Racing Minister Tim Mander and Child Safety Minister Amanda Camm.

In correspondence reported over the weekend and seen by the ABC, the sister-in-law of Mr Mander, Lynne Waters, wrote to Premier David Crisafulli on June 27 last year, alleging Mr Mander and Ms Camm had been maintaining an affair for two years prior to Mr Mander's 2025 divorce from his wife.

Ms Waters asked the premier whether the relationship was disclosed prior to their appointments as ministers, expressing concern the Ministerial Code of Conduct may have been had been breached.

The code requires that ministers declare personal relationships that could lead to a conflict of interest.

Mr Mander said his relationship with Ms Camm ended before their appointments and later resumed. (AAP: Darren England)

In a statement, Mr Mander said his personal relationship with Ms Camm began in June, 2023, and ended in May, 2024, before later resuming.

"We were categorically not in a relationship when we were sworn in as ministers in late 2024," he said.

This timeline was supported by Ms Camm, who said in a statement she had "complied with integrity advice and conflict management plans" and that correct disclosures had been made.

"The personal nature of reporting on this matter has been distressing and I ask that my family's privacy is respected," she said.

Timeline under scrutiny

In a follow-up email to Mr Crisafulli's office, Ms Waters noted the relationship between Mr Mander and Ms Camm was officially disclosed at a cabinet meeting on July 14, 2025 — about two weeks after her first email was sent.

She stated she had evidence "indicating that this relationship has been ongoing for a minimum of two years prior to my correspondence".

In his statement issued on Monday, Mr Mander said he made the decision to separate from his wife in April, 2025, and recommenced his relationship with Ms Camm in June, ahead of the July cabinet disclosure.

"I immediately sought advice from the Integrity Commissioner and the Clerk of the Parliament and made all the necessary declarations in line with that advice and the Ministerial Code of Conduct," he said.

Queensland LNP's Deputy Leader Tim Mander has bowed out of the leadership race to replace Deb Frecklington. (ABC News: Timothy Swanston)

"I have also written to the Electoral Commission of Queensland to affirm that I followed the appropriate processes to put the matter to rest.

" My focus remains on my electorate and ministerial responsibilities and delivering for Queenslanders. "

He said it had been a "deeply personal and challenging time".

'Ross and Rachel' accusations from Labor

Speaking on Sunday, Mr Crisafulli said both ministers had assured him everything was above-board.

"Both are aware entirely about what their duties are. Both believe that it was declared entirely at the right time," he said.

David Crisafulli says anyone with concerns or evidence can refer the matter to the CCC. (ABC News: Charlie McLean)

He urged anyone with concerns or evidence of wrongdoing to refer the matter to the Crime and Corruption Commission.

"[I've done] the same thing I do with all my ministers, make sure they're well aware of the Ministerial Code of Conduct and how important it is that you comply with it, to make sure that you do declare the things that have to be declared," he said.

"And if there is a suggestion that that hasn't happened, I'll be the first to say that it should be referred and it should be tested, and that is the advice I'd give to the Labor party or to any individuals."

Deputy opposition leader Cameron Dick said Mr Crisafulli wanted "Queenslanders to believe that Tim Mander and Amanda Camm are the Ross and Rachel of his government".

Cameron Dick says the government should release the integrity advice and conflict of interest management plans. (ABC News)

"Tim Mander and Amanda Camm say they were in a relationship for about a year. Then they weren't. Then they were," Mr Dick said.

" And very conveniently, the time that they say that their relationship recommenced was almost exactly the same day that Tim Mander's sister-in-law wrote to the premier raising serious concerns about that relationship and the proper exercise of ministerial duties by those two ministers. "

Mr Dick said Mr Crisafulli should detail what he knew and when he first knew it, urging the government to release the integrity advice and conflict of interest management plans provided to Mr Mander and Ms Camm.

title: Kasatkina wins first tournament as an Australian citizen
text:
Daria Kasatkina has won her first tournament since becoming an Australian citizen.

Kasatkina defeated Germany's Tamara Korpatsch 2-6, 6-3, 7-5 in a marathon Catalonia Open final in Vic, Spain.

The clay-court tournament is a second-tier WTA 125 event.

Kostyuk claims Madrid Open victory Ukraine's Marta Kostyuk backflips after beating Russian Mirra Andreeva in the Madrid Open final.

WTA 125 events are considered the equivalent of men's ATP Challenger tournaments.

But for Kasatkina, the triumph came after she slumped to her lowest world ranking for 11 years.

She entered the tournament at 75 on the WTA standings, a far cry from her career-best ranking of eight, which she achieved in 2022.

"Can't describe how happy I am to lift this trophy after a long time of struggle," Kasatkina wrote on Instagram.

"Thanks everyone for your support."

Russia-born Kasatkina switched allegiance to Australia last year.

She became an Australian citizen in January.

AAP

title: Small business faces $500,000 bill to go electric amid gas switch-off
text:
An Albany restaurateur says he is facing costs of half a million dollars to switch over to electrical appliances after the state's gas provider said it would disconnect the city from reticulated liquid petroleum gas (LPG).

ATCO Australia plans to decommission its Albany gas distribution system, citing "excessive costs" to replace its aging network.

The state's gas provider told 8,000 customers it would begin disconnecting gas lines later this year in a staged process over the next three years.

Affected residents and businesses have been encouraged to switch to other energy sources or transition to bottled LPG.

Les Palmer says the cost to switch from gas to electricity is too high. (ABC Great Southern: Andrew Chounding)

Infrastructure blow for 'big city'

Albany steakhouse owner Les Palmer said the cost to upgrade wiring and electrical appliances in his restaurant was too high.

"The vast majority of my equipment uses gas, and the ability to change to electric is not impossible, but it's vastly out of my ability to pay … between $150,000 to $300,000 to upgrade the power alone," he said.

"And then you've got to do the equipment change, which is close to another $200,000."

Les Palmer says the cost to replace appliances in his Albany steakhouse is estimated at $200,000. (ABC Great Southern: Andrew Chounding)

Mr Palmer criticised the decommissioning of the city's gas network as short-sighted.

"Here we are, the second biggest growing town in Western Australia, and we're all going off reticulated to just bottle gas everywhere," he said.

He said moving to bottled LPG also brought additional challenges.

"I need to get a gas tanker to come to the restaurant two to three times a week to keep filling me up … if for some reason there's a disruption, I could be without gas and without the ability to operate."

ATCO develops transition plan

Hayden Jones, who runs a laundromat in the Albany CBD that relies heavily on LPG, said ATCO's announcement had created uncertainty for his business.

Hayden Jones says ATCO Australia's decision has brought uncertainty for some businesses. (ABC Great Southern: Andrew Chounding)

"Obviously, it's going to involve … permissions from the landlord, and, I presume, Albany City with planning, because you can't just stick gas bottles anywhere you like," he said.

" There are certain rules and regulations … we're still looking at how we're going to do that. "

On its website, ATCO says it is developing a transition plan in collaboration with the state government and industry partners, and would provide more information to customers before they were disconnected.

Customers have been told no immediate action is required.

Albany Mayor Greg Stocks said the decision to decommission the city's gas network had been imposed without adequate engagement or planning.

Greg Stocks says suitable compensation for residents is needed. (ABC Great Southern: Andrew Chounding)

Mr Stocks said the city had asked the state government to push back any transition to 2027, and provide compensation for all affected customers, after Energy Minister Amber Jade Sanderson announced $10.8 million would be provided to convert the region's social housing and plan for the transition.

"The scale of this transition means we must ensure that support is extended more broadly, so households and businesses outside the social housing system are not carrying a financial burden," he said.

"Residents and businesses need certainty, financial support, and time to make informed decisions."

Greg Stocks wants full compensation for residents transitioning to LPG gas bottles. (ABC Great Southern: Andrew Chounding)

Mr Stocks said the city wanted full financial compensation for those transitioning to bottled gas, a 75 per cent subsidy for those switching to electricity, and consideration for residents and businesses facing unique and complex circumstances.

Ms Sanderson said a working group had been set up to hear the concerns of affected customers.

"We are working collaboratively with the City of Albany and the broader working group on the decommissioning of ATCO gas," she said.

"We recognise that as we move through the decommissioning, there may be additional costs, which will be considered through normal processes. We also expect ATCO to contribute to that cost."

ATCO said it would work with the state government and advise customers what support was included in the transition when it became available.

title: Aussie rugby sevens star joins NRLW Titans in 'huge coup'
text:
Australian rugby sevens star Teagan Levi will play for the Gold Coast Titans in the 2026 NRLW season.

Levi will link up with the Titans following her Rugby Australia commitments.

The ex-Miami State High School student began her professional sporting career with the Gold Coast Suns in the AFLW.

She has been a member of the Australian rugby sevens squad for the past six years.

"I want to test myself and keep growing as a person and a player, and this opportunity allows me to step out of my comfort zone," Levi said in a Titans statement.

"Making the switch to league has always been on my mind.

"But I wanted to make sure I was able to be all-in and giving 100 per cent to Murph (Titans coach Karyn Murphy) and the rest of my Titans teammates."

Murphy said Levi was a valuable addition to the Titans squad.

"It's a huge coup to have Teagan return to her hometown and join us for the 2026 season," she said.

"She's been on the world stage wearing the green and gold in significant tournaments the past few years and her big-game experience and exposure to high-performance environments will be valuable for our upcoming campaign.

"I'm looking forward to working with her in the preseason ahead of round one."

Levi and her sister Maddison are among the highest-profile players in the Australian rugby sevens program.

The Titans finished ninth in the 12-team NRLW premiership last year.

The 2026 NRLW season begins on July 2.

title: Agriculture sector sees 'silver lining' from global disruptions
text:
Australia's agricultural peak body believes the recent surge in media attention, driven by global trade disruptions and cost pressures, is putting farming firmly back on the national agenda.

National Farmers' Federation (NFF) president Hamish McIntyre says the increased contact from the media and government to his office in the past couple of months has allowed key NFF goals, such as boosting domestic manufacturing capacity, to be front and centre.

Hamish McIntyre says the attention has allowed farming advocacy to "cut through". (Supplied: National Farmers' Federation)

"We've had doors that have been shut for a long time reopened," Mr McIntyre says.

"I'll receive calls from ministers on Saturdays to get our thoughts again, which is great. It's finally hit home that we all need to work very closely together to get the long-term policy settings right.

"The media attention has really highlighted to all of Australians – particularly in metropolitan Australia – how connected we as farmers are to them.

"Without it, it's very hard to get the cut-though. It's got everyone working together."

Publicist Georgie Robertson says she is seeing a more sustained interest in the sector. (Supplied)

The Regional PR Co's Georgie Robertson said the sector was experiencing an exceptional surge in media attention, creating a key opportunity for farmers to directly share their stories.

"We've got this perfect storm happening right now where we've got global trade disruption; we've got cost-of-living pressures; we've got food security concerns, and they've all kind of landed at the same time," she said.

Ms Robertson said issues in the sector that were previously perceived as complex were now being picked up as part of the vernacular by mainstream media.

The issue of access to urea, a key ingredient for agricultural fertilisers, was in the foreground of media coverage when Prime Minister Anthony Albanese flew to Brunei to shore up its supply to Australia.

"Suddenly our city mates are using the words 'supply chain' and 'urea' in one sentence," she said.

"I think suddenly ag isn't a rural or regional bush story anymore. It's an economic story. It's a political story. It's a story about what's in your supermarket trolley. It's a story that's ours to tell."

Anthony Albanese recently flew to Brunei to secure Australia's urea supply. (AAP: Bianca De Marchi)

Ms Robertson said her phone was ringing more than ever, with enduring interest from city journalists keen to interview farmers and better understand agricultural systems.

"There's less of a dramatic spike and more of a sustained interest in talking about ag.

" There's an exciting opportunity here where we can create a generation of urban Australians who genuinely get where their food comes from, long past the news cycle that we're experiencing at the moment. "

Farmers urged to tell their story

Ms Robertson encourages farmers to join the national conversation and take advantage of the momentum building in the press.

"I think the worst thing you can do is sit back and let other people tell your story," she said.

"Not the industry spokespeople or government, but actual farmers who are living the day-to-day reality.

"The door is open, so we, as an industry, now have to kind of walk through it."

GrainGrowers CEO Shona Gawel describes the media attention as a "silver lining" to the supply chain challenges created by the war in the Middle East.

Shona Gawel says she hopes the attention will bring awareness to issues faced by the growers. (Supplied)

"Things we've been asking for, for a long time, like ensuring Australia has a more sustainable agricultural future, are being listened to really carefully now," she said.

"When you've got the prime minister going overseas in different markets to talk about securing fertiliser, that's really positive."

In WA's central Wheatbelt, Wyalkatchem grain grower Owen Garner is pleased about the attention the sector is getting.

He said he had previously been concerned about the divide between rural and urban understandings of the food cycle.

Owen Garner hopes media attention will spur on interest in agriculture. (ABC Midwest and Wheatbelt: Eliza Bidstrup)

"Now you switch on the radio or TV and farming is being talked about," Mr Garner said.

"We [farmers] are an important part of the cycle and the positive out of this might be that people who have let their understanding of agriculture lapse might actually prick their ears up and be a bit more interested."

title: Three die after suspected hantavirus outbreak on cruise ship
text:
A suspected hantavirus outbreak on a cruise ship in the Atlantic Ocean has killed three people and at least three others have needed medical attention, according to the World Health Organization (WHO).

The WHO said in a statement that an investigation was underway, but that at least one case of hantavirus had been confirmed.

Humans can catch hantaviruses through contact with infected mice or rats or their droppings, being bitten or by inhaling contaminated dust.

What's the illness that killed Gene Hackman's wife? Betsy Arakawa, wife of actor Gene Hackman, died of a hantavirus infection a full week before her husband. Medical experts have said it has a low mortality rate and was mostly spread by rodents.

There are multiple types of hantaviruses in different parts of the world, with different symptoms.

The WHO said, while rare, hantavirus could spread between people and lead to severe respiratory illness.

There is no specific treatment or cure, but early medical attention can increase the chance of survival.

The late actor Gene Hackman's wife, Betsy Arakawa, died from a hantavirus infection in the US last year, and Hackman died a week later at their home.

'Important not to panic'

Professor Meru Sheel, an infectious diseases epidemiologist from Sydney University, said the news of the outbreak on a cruise ship was surprising.

"Any large outbreaks on a cruise ship will tend to evoke a reaction in people, a memory of what happened with COVID-19, but it's important to not panic as yet," she said.

Professor Sheel said rodents naturally carried different hantaviruses but they were not transmitted from human to human.

Exhausted passengers arrive home after luxury cruise liner runs aground About 80 passengers on the Coral Adventurer are flown home after the cruise ship ran aground on a reef off Papua New Guinea.

She said the virus presented in two key ways, with very severe respiratory infections, showing symptoms like chills, fevers, headaches and nausea, and with haemorrhagic fever, which could be fatal.

Professor Sheel said she had no knowledge of cases in humans in Australia and while there were questions regarding exposure on the ship, the risk of the virus spreading wide, like a pandemic, was low.

One of the patients from the cruise ship was in intensive care in a South African hospital, the UN health agency said, adding that it was working with authorities to evacuate two other passengers with symptoms from the ship.

"Detailed investigations are ongoing, including further laboratory testing, and epidemiological investigations," the WHO said.

"Medical care and support are being provided to passengers and crew.

"Sequencing of the virus is also ongoing."

Hantavirus outbreak on cruise ship

The WHO did not identify the vessel, but South African media reports said the outbreak happened on the MV Hondius cruise ship while it was sailing from Argentina to Cape Verde, off the coast of West Africa.

The MarineTraffic global shipping website identifies the vessel as a Dutch-flagged passenger cruise ship.

It can accommodate about 170 passengers and has some 70 crew members.

It is listed as a polar cruise ship on the websites of several travel agencies and it is operated by a Dutch-based tour company, Oceanwide Expeditions.

One of the cruises offers an itinerary departing from Ushuaia for Cape Verde, with stops in the islands of South Georgia and Saint Helena.

According to several online ship-tracking sites, the MV Hondius was just off the port of Praia, the capital of Cape Verde, on Sunday.

Evacuations to Johannesburg

South African media, quoting South African health department spokesperson Foster Mohale, said the first victim, an elderly man, died on the ship.

The first person to develop symptoms was a 70-year-old passenger, whose body was currently on the island of Saint Helena, a British territory in the South Atlantic, Mr Mohale said.

The passenger's 69-year-old wife also fell ill on board and was evacuated to South Africa, where she died in a Johannesburg hospital, Mr Mohale said, adding that they were not yet able to confirm the victims' nationalities.

The third case, a 69-year-old Briton, was also evacuated to Johannesburg, where he was being treated in intensive care.

A source close to the case, speaking on condition of anonymity, said a Dutch couple were among the dead. The body of the third person to die was still on board the ship.

Judge blocks release of Gene Hackman autopsy documents The New Mexico ruling blocks the release of any images showing the bodies of Gene Hackman or Betsy Arakawa.

Discussions were underway to decide whether two other sick passengers should be placed in isolation in hospital in Cape Verde, after which the ship would continue to Spain's Canary Islands, the anonymous source said.

The WHO said it was "facilitating coordination" between national authorities and the ship's operators to organise the medical evacuation of two passengers with symptoms.

The ABC has sent questions to cruise operator Oceanwide Expeditions but has not yet had a reply.

AP/AFP

title: Cummins endures horror IPL over as Sunrisers fall to Knight Riders
text:
Pat Cummins conceded a staggering 27 runs in one over during Sunrisers Hyderabad's Indian Premier League (IPL) loss to Kolkata Knight Riders.

New Zealand's Finn Allen pummelled Cummins for 22 of those runs during the Sunrisers captain's second over in Hyderabad.

Cummins did have the last laugh, however, as he dismissed Allen for 29 when the opener was caught by Heinrich Klaasen at deep midwicket.

The Knight Riders went on to record a seven-wicket victory, ending the Sunrisers' five-match winning streak.

"Not our best day," said Cummins, who finished with 1-47 from his four overs.

Starc takes three on IPL return Mitchell Starc picked up three wickets on his first bowl in this year's Indian Premier League, as Marnus Labuschagne helped Hyderabad United to the Pakistan Super League final.

"The margins are quite small and if I hadn't gone for about 30 in one over at the start, it might have been different as well.

"Those little moments can make a big difference."

The Knight Riders finished 3-169, reaching the victory target with 10 balls to spare.

Allen made 29, while Angkrish Raghuvanshi top-scored with 59.

It was the Knight Riders' third straight win.

Earlier, Travis Head bludgeoned 61 off 28 for the Sunrisers, who were all out for 165.

Head, who opened the batting with Abhishek Sharma (15), hit nine fours and three sixes.

Fellow Australian Cameron Green took 1-34 for the Knight Riders.

AAP

title: Parents of 14yo athlete who died suddenly work to prevent future heartbreaks
text:
A go-getter, respectful, kind and always smiling. That's how the people who knew Josh Oguns remember him.

And while he had many achievements, above anything else, the Canberra teenager was known for his love of sport.

"He was very committed to it; he would train and train hard. He was a really disciplined athlete," his mother, Taiye Oguns, said.

His main passion was soccer, but he had also been involved in touch football, athletics, martial arts and basketball.

Josh Oguns suffered a sudden cardiac arrest during a basketball game. (Supplied)

What started as a normal day of Saturday sport in July last year ended in tragedy.

After playing soccer earlier in the day, Josh suffered a sudden cardiac arrest during a basketball game.

He died 13 days later at just 14 years old.

"We miss him a lot," Ms Oguns said.

"Joshua was just an easy-going boy, always happy and always just very helpful.

"Not having him in that space is really quite difficult but we are taking one day at a time."

Josh Oguns was a passionate soccer player. (Supplied)

Cardiac screening encouraged

During efforts to save his life, doctors discovered Josh had an undiagnosed heart condition: an abnormal blood supply to the left side of his heart.

"There was no sign, no sign whatsoever," Josh's father, Joe Oguns, said.

"No young person should die from doing what they love."

Joe and Taiye Oguns have established a charity dedicated to keeping young athletes safe from sudden cardiac arrest. (ABC News: Toby Hunt)

Josh's parents are now on a mission to help make sure no other family experiences the same pain.

They've established a charity, Josh4Hearts Foundation, dedicated to keeping young athletes safe from sudden cardiac arrest.

"We are hoping that this can be an opportunity to raise awareness and help other families [so] hopefully they will not have to go through what we have had to endure," Ms Oguns said.

Josh Oguns was known for his love of sports. (Supplied)

The first aim of the foundation is to encourage sporting clubs and bodies to refer their young players for cardiac screening.

The foundation's broader aim is to support local communities and sporting clubs to respond effectively to sudden cardiac arrest during games and training.

"Every single second counts because the first 3 minutes are the most critical time … if you are waiting for the ambulance to arrive, it's probably too late," Mr Oguns said.

" Cardiac arrest in an athlete is a highly survivable event if you know how to recognise it and what to do. "

'Really ambitious venture'

While young athletes are often considered the healthiest in society, one in 300 carry a chance of having a potentially dangerous underlying heart condition, according to Macquarie University associate professor Hari Raju.

He said while many professional sporting codes screened their players for potential heart problems, the same couldn't be said about amateur sport.

"There is very little, if any, in fact I am not aware of any program like this in the country outside of professional athletes," Dr Raju said.

"This is a really ambitious venture in the Australian landscape."

Hari Raju says one in 300 young athletes carry a chance of having a potentially dangerous underlying heart condition. (Supplied)

He said while it was "rare" for an athlete to suffer from sudden cardiac arrest, significant work needed to be done to limit the risk.

"If somebody collapses, outside the context of a head injury, then you should assume that that individual has had a cardiac arrest," he said.

"Individual sporting clubs investing in basic life support training for all of their players and all of their coaches … that's a really simple intervention."

Dr Raju said there had been some high-profile instances of sudden cardiac arrest in sport, including soccer player Christian Eriksen who suffered an on-field cardiac arrest.

"The kind of thing we are looking at here is to try and ensure that amateur athletes have access to a similar level of support," he said.

title: Quick hits: Gasly flips out, Verstappen spins and F1 could go back to V8s
text:
After a five-week hiatus Formula 1 made up for lost time in Miami.

Kimi Antonelli won a third consecutive grand prix and was able to avoid the chaos that took place behind him.

Meanwhile, there is already talk about what could replace F1's V6 hybrid engines, just four weekends into the new regulation cycle.

Here are the quick hits from the Formula 1 Miami Grand Prix.

1. FIA boss says V8 engines are coming back

Formula 1's new era of engines has not been a big hit so far and the FIA president says the age of electrical power could be coming to an end.

F1 has used V6 hybrid engines since 2014, but the current iteration is the first to have its power come from a near 50-50 split between internal combustion and electrical power.

The engines are starved of energy by design and require drivers to constantly harvest power throughout the lap to ensure the power unit operates at an optimal level.

F1 changes rules over engine concerns The next Formula 1 race in Miami will be a testing and proving ground for a number of new rules aimed at improving performance and safety.

Many drivers have spoken out against the need to harvest energy, taking away from their ability to drive flat out.

The president of motorsport's governing body has suggested F1 could revert to V8 engines at the end of the current regulation cycle.

"It's coming. At the end of the day, it's a matter of time," Mohammed Ben Sulayem told reporters in Miami.

"In 2031, the V8, the FIA will have the power to do it, without any votes from the PUMs [Power Unit Manufacturers]. That's the regulations. But we want to bring it one year earlier, which everyone now is asking for."

FIA president Mohammed Ben Sulayem wants F1 to have V8 engines. (Reuters: Brian Snyder)

It is not the first time Ben Sulayem has touted his desire for F1 to move away from engines that are heavily reliant on electric power.

F1's use of hybrid engines reflects its prevalence in regular road cars, while also being part of the sport's pledge to be net zero on carbon emissions by 2030.

"You get the sound, less complexity, lightweight," Ben Sulayem said of V8 engines.

"You will hear about it very soon and it will be with a very, very minor electrification.

"I'm positive, they want it to happen. But let's say the manufacturers don't approve it [for 2030]. The next year, it will happen. In 2031 it's done anyway. It will be done. V8 is coming."

The debate between the V6 hybrids or going back to V8 engines with "very, very minor electrification" looks set to continue for years.

2. Russell, Verstappen and Leclerc battle to the line

Sometimes you get a great battle on the final lap for a minor placing. But it's not every race that three cars are battling in the final corners for fourth place.

However, thanks to a big mistake from Charles Leclerc, that is what fans got to enjoy at the conclusion of the Miami Grand Prix.

Leclerc held the lead early, but ultimately his Ferrari was no match for Kimi Antonelli and Lando Norris.

Leclerc was desperately holding onto third place in the closing stages of the race, but lost the position to Australian Oscar Piastri on the penultimate lap.

Leclerc tried to fight back, but his car spun and clipped the wall.

That ended his hopes of third, and ultimately cost him more.

Leclerc was nursing his injured car to the finish line, as George Russell and Max Verstappen mowed him down.

Russell passed him on the back straight before the final braking zone of the lap.

Leclerc tried to fight back, but clipped the rear of Russell's Mercedes.

Russell was clear, but Leclerc was vulnerable to Verstappen who pounced on his chance.

The Red Bull driver stuck his nose up the inside and then won the drag race to the chequered flag. It was a hectic finish to a race filled with drama.

Leclerc would be handed a 20-second penalty after the race for deliberately cutting corners on the final lap, dropping him from sixth to eighth.

3. Gasly flipped on his head

There were plenty of flashpoints during the Miami Grand Prix, but none were as stunning as Pierre Gasly's race-ending crash.

The Frenchman had just passed New Zealander Liam Lawson for 10th place on lap six of 57.

But Lawson wanted to take the place back immediately.

Lawson stuck the nose of his Racing Bull up the inside of Gasly's Alpine but clipped the rear wheel.

That contact sent the Alpine flipping over itself, before resting on the tyre barrier.

Despite being dramatic viewing, Gasly was able to exit the car under his own power and did not appear to sustain any injury.

4. Verstappen tries to save time with a 360

Even when he makes a mistake, Max Verstappen is still able to show why he is one of the finest drivers F1 has ever had.

Starting on the front row, Verstappen had a chance to lead on the opening lap when pole sitter Kimi Antonelli locked his wheels and went into the run-off area.

But Verstappen also had a lock-up, which allowed Charles Leclerc to swoop and take the lead.

Verstappen tried to fight, but he lost control of his car and went spinning.

That was where Verstappen's mistake turned to brilliance.



"I lost the rear into turn 2 and then tried to minimise the time loss with a 360," he told Sky F1.

Knowing he was going to lose time no matter what, Verstappen chose to ensure his car was pointing in the right direction as fast as possible.

To achieve this, the Dutchman kept his foot down and completed a 360-spin, and then carried on in one motion.

He lost ground, but saved so much time by being able to spin his car in traffic and get it pointing in the right direction.

It was a move that left former F1 driver Martin Brundle in awe.

"That was genius from Verstappen," Brundle said.

"Absolutely genius."

5. Antonelli joins elite company after his triple treat

So far this year, Kimi Antonelli has become the youngest grand prix winner ever and the youngest driver to lead the Formula 1 drivers' championship.

In Miami, he continued his historic run by qualifying for the grand prix on pole position and winning the race.

ABC Sport Daily podcast ABC Sport Daily is your daily sports conversation. We dive into the biggest story of the day and get you up to speed with everything else that's making headlines.

Antonelli became the third driver in history to have his first three grand prix poles in consecutive races. The other two drivers on that list are Ayrton Senna and Michael Schumacher.

However, Antonelli has done something Senna and Schumacher could not. He won all three races.

Antonelli has also had his first three F1 grand prix victories in consecutive races. Only Damon Hill and Mika Häkkinen have also achieved that.

"This is just the beginning, the road is still long," he said after the race.

"We're working super hard and the team is doing an incredible job. Without them, I wouldn't be here."

The list of accomplishments the 19-year-old is achieving is impressive, and he is showing no signs of slowing down.

title: Hull takes heavy fall in dramatic sprint finish
text:
Hull takes heavy fall in dramatic sprint finish

Jessica Hull is clipped by Claudia Hollingsworth and hits the deck in a wild sprint finish in the women's 1500m final at the Australian athletics championships.

title: Australia news LIVE: Iran warns Trump’s ‘Project Freedom’ on Strait of Hormuz would break ceasefire; Chalmers flags intergenerational reform in federal budget
text:
Viva Energy says repairs to the processing unit damaged in a major blaze at its Geelong refinery will take about six weeks to complete.

The fire that broke out three weeks ago in the alkylation unit, which converts gases into a component needed in petrol, was sparked by an equipment fault and reduced production of petrol from the refinery on the shores of Corio Bay to 60 per cent.

The blaze at the refinery on April 16. Jessika Louise Wicks/FRV GEELONG

In a statement today, Viva Energy said it expected the affected unit to be repaired and returned to operation in June, with petrol production to return to 90 per cent of capacity once work is completed.

Jet fuel and diesel at the refinery are still being produced at about 80 per cent of capacity. When running at near full capacity, the refinery supplies about 50 per cent of all fuel in Victoria and about 10 per cent of fuel used nationally.

“The company continues to have sufficient fuel stocks to cover this reduced production and expects to maintain normal fuel supply to our customers throughout this period,” Viva said.

Before the blaze, Viva was seeking to produce as much fuel as possible to help the country deal with the oil shock sparked by the war in the Middle East and closure of the Strait of Hormuz.

title: Gateway tracks 4km strike at WA Yandal gold camp
text:
Advertisement

Sponsored BusinessCompaniesBulls N' Bears Gateway tracks 4km strike at WA Yandal gold camp Brought to you by BULLS N’ BEARS Murray Ward May 4, 2026 — 1:39pm Save You have reached your maximum number of saved items. Remove items from your saved list to add more. Share A A A

Gateway Mining has significantly expanded the footprint of its Yandal gold project, 85 Kilometres northeast of Wiluna in Western Australia. The company has traced shallow, oxide gold mineralisation over a massive 4-kilometre strike at its Haflinger prospect. The latest end-of-hole results from air core drilling along the Celia-Mustang trend have also confirmed high-grade primary gold at Gateway’s Hummer prospect, suggesting the company could be sitting on a major new gold camp in the heart of WA. Drill operators look over drilling plans at Gateway Mining’s Yandal gold project in Western Australia. The main takeaway from the latest campaign has been the hefty size emerging at Haflinger. The results have extended the known high-grade mineralisation by 220 metres to the south, pushing the total strike length of the oxide gold zone to 4 kilometres along the Celia Shear structure. Notable hits from the company’s southward Haflinger extension drilling included 12 metres running at 1.1 grams per tonne(g/t) gold from 104 metres, with a further hole landing 8 metres at 1.1g/t gold from 96 metres. Interestingly, the prospect is cut and offset by a northeast-trending brittle structure, where another hole intercepted 4 metres grading 1.4g/t gold from only 48 metres.

Advertisement

‘These latest results from the Celia-Mustang Trend are another strong step forward.’ Gateway Mining chief executive officer Richard Pugh Gateway says the latest results have backed up a previous monster intercept of 64 metres at 1.2g/t gold from 56 metres, which featured 24 metres at 2.4g/t gold, underscoring the significant potential for stacked lodes within the broader structural corridor. While Haflinger provides the scale, the company’s adjacent Hummer prospect is delivering some high-grade heat of its own from the primary rock. The prospect sits on a splay structure off the Celia Shear and was initially identified through a 16-metre hit at 1.0 g/t gold from 64 metres. The recent drilling results appear to have unearthed a primary gold lode more than 200 metres long beneath a broader 700-metre oxide zone. Crucially, the latest two holes at Hummer both finished in high-grade primary mineralisation at the bottom of the hole, with the system wide open at depth. Standout results included 6 metres at 2.2 g/t gold from 80 metres to bottom-of-hole and 5 metres at 1.7 g/t gold from 100 metres to bottom-of-hole. The results seem to validate Gateway’s theory that the Celia-Mustang structural corridor contains a series of repeating, high-grade gold lodes.

Advertisement

The company says this mirrors the common geological setting of major gold systems found throughout the Yilgarn Craton. The major characteristic being shear zones that flex around the margins of intrusions. Gateway Mining chief executive officer Richard Pugh said: “These latest results from the Celia-Mustang Trend are another strong step forward in what is rapidly emerging as a significant new gold camp.” Beyond the immediate success at Haflinger and Hummer, Gateway is maintaining momentum across its 1780-square-kilometre patch at Yandal. The company is waiting on a significant batch of assays from an 18,000-metre drilling program 15 kilometres to the northwest of the Celia-Mustang trend at its Great Western prospect. First results are expected to land in the next two weeks. The company says the highly anticipated results should complement the current Yandal global resource of 8.17 million tonnes grading 1.52 g/t for 400,400 ounces of gold, currently centred on its Horse Well and Dusk ’til Dawn prospects. With a formidable war chest of A$15.7 million in cash and A$5.6 million in liquid securities, Gateway is well-funded to continue accelerating its exploration program. The next steps will involve systematic reverse circulation drilling to delineate the plunge and geometry of the newly identified lodes at Haflinger and Hummer, where the primary mineralisation in fresh rock remains largely untested.

Advertisement

Gateway’s big system play at Yandal is starting to show its teeth, with four kilometres of strike already secured and high-grade gold potentially lurking at depth. Punters are likely to be laser-focused on a soon-to-be-delivered torrent of assays from Great Western while watching for more results from the spinning drill rods at Haflinger and Hummer. title: ASX slides lower; ASIC probes Accent CEO’s share trading; a2Milk recall
text:
Advertisement

Updated BusinessMarketsWorld markets ASX slides lower; ASIC probes Accent CEO’s share trading; a2Milk recall Staff writers Updated May 4, 2026 — 1:23pm ,first published May 4, 2026 — 5:19am Save You have reached your maximum number of saved items. Remove items from your saved list to add more. Share A A A

The Australian sharemarket remains in the red at lunchtime as uncertainty over the Iran war lingers, while there were sharp falls for retailer Accent Group and dairy company a2 Milk. The S&P/ASX 200 was 32.1 points, or 0.4 per cent lower to 8697.7, with five of 11 industry sectors in positive territory, led by technology stocks. The main game this week is Tuesday’s call on interest rates by the Reserve Bank, with the central bank tipped to announce another hike. Wall Street continues to rise despite the uncertainty of the Iran war. AP Accent Group tumbled 12.6 per cent as the operator of shoe retailers Platypus and Hype said the Australian Securities and Investments Commission (ASIC) is investigating possible breaches of the law in relation to trading in Accent shares last year. Accent told the ASX the company had been required to preserve communications concerning the chief executive Daniel Agostinelli, non-executive director Michael Hapgood, and another senior employee of the company.

Advertisement

Accent said the investigation relates to trading between May 23 and June 10 last year - the company told the market last year that an entity linked to Agostinelli had sold shares in that period. Accent said on Monday that no charges had been laid and there were no allegations against the company. “Mr Agostinelli’s on-market share sales were pre-approved by the former chair of Accent at the relevant time. Mr Agostinelli has the full support of the board in his ongoing role as CEO,” the company said. ASIC did not comment. National Australia Bank slumped 1.9 per cent after its first-half cash earnings came in below expectations, falling 26.3 per cent in annual terms, to $2.6 billion, weighed down by a $949 billion hit to profit due to changes in the bank’s accounting policies on software. Excluding the hit from these accounting changes, the bank said cash earnings rose 2.3 per cent compared with the September half.

Advertisement

Chief executive Andrew Irvine said the economic backdrop was more volatile because of geopolitical tensions, and the bank had raised its provisions for bad debts. Commonwealth Bank dipped 0.5 per cent, Westpac slid 0.3 per cent while ANZ Bank was 0.7 per cent higher in midday trade. Dairy company a2Milk tumbled 11.8 per cent after it announced it is recalling batches of a product sold in the US after detecting a toxin linked to vomiting and nausea. Three batches of a2 Platinum USA-label formula have been recalled after manufacturer Synlait Milk identified the presence of cereulide, a2 said on Monday. The recall impacts just the US and not the company’s most lucrative market in China. The recall is in relation to a relatively small quantity of three batches comprising 63,078 tins of which an estimated 16,428 tins were sold to consumers, the company said. No confirmed incidents of infant illness or harm have been reported, it said. The main uncertainty for the global economy is where oil prices are heading because of the Iran war. President Donald Trump said on Monday (AEST) that the US would begin guiding neutral ships out through the Strait of Hormuz and signalled progress on talks with Iran to end the war now in its 10th week. Oil prices edged lower in response in early Asian trade, with Brent, the international standard, down 0.3 per cent to $US108.09 ($150.08) per barrel while US oil lost 0.3 per cent to $US101.66 at 12.17pm AEST. The slide in oil prices sent local energy stocks lower with Woodside Energy losing 3 per cent while Santos retreated 2.2 per cent. Refiners Ampol and Viva Energy each lost 1.6 per cent.

Advertisement

Mining stocks are steady with BHP adding 0.5 per cent and Fortescue 0.1 per cent while fellow iron ore heavyweight Fortescue edged 0.1 per cent lower. Gold stocks are mixed with Northern Star sliding 0.1 per cent and Evolution Mining adding 0.6 per cent. Technology stocks are higher, with tracking app Life360 jumping 7.1 per cent, while Xero rose 2.4 per cent and Technology One 1.2 per cent. WiseTech shed 1.3 per cent. Southern Cross Media was 1.7 per cent higher after chair Heith Mackay-Cruise announced his intention to resign on Monday morning after an influential shareholder called for him and two other directors to step down on Friday. Teresa Dyson, a director from Kerry Stokes’ old publishing empire Seven West Media, will be the new chair of the radio network that absorbed the media mogul’s former TV and publishing company in January. The merger, which had been sold as a merger of equals, quickly began to resemble a takeover by Southern Cross after Mackay-Cruise ousted a number of Seven-aligned senior staff and became executive chairman. That period is now ending. The Australian dollar was trading at US72.04¢ at 12.38pm AEST. The US stock market set more records on Friday after Apple and others joined the list of companies delivering fatter profits for the start of the year than analysts expected.

Advertisement

Related Article Opinion

Cybersecurity The AI craze is a double-edged sword for our banking giants Clancy Yeates Deputy business editor Apple led the way after the iPhone seller reported stronger profit and revenue for the latest quarter than analysts expected. Because it’s one of Wall Street’s biggest stocks in terms of overall size, Apple’s rally of 3.3 per cent was by far the strongest force lifting the S&P 500. The S&P 500 climbed 0.3 per cent to its latest all-time high and closed out a fifth-straight winning week. That’s its longest such streak since 2024. The Dow Jones dipped 152 points, or 0.3 per cent, and the Nasdaq composite added 0.9 per cent to its own record. US companies have been blowing past expectations for earnings in the first three months of 2026. That’s even with the war with Iran and high oil prices souring confidence for many US households. A little more than a quarter of the companies in the S&P 500 have reported already, and 84 per cent of them have topped analysts’ estimates, according to FactSet. The index is on track to deliver roughly 15 per cent growth in profit from a year earlier.
